preamble: Primary palatalization (iotation) describes the process where a palatalized alveolar stop shifts its primary place and manner of articulation to become a postalveolar affricate. This change involves the tongue blade making broader contact further back in the mouth, transitioning from a simple stop to a complex affricate sound.

examples.note: Common verb mutation where dental 't' followed by 'j' or front vowels yields 'ch' in present tense.
Latin [tj] clusters developed into various dental/postalveolar affricates across the Romance domain.
Productive yod-coalescence in casual speech where /t/ + /j/ merges into /tʃ/.

sources: Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
